nativeRange.habitat: The Firehead Tetra is native to blackwater rivers and flooded forest tributaries, where the water is tannin-stained, slow-moving, and tea-colored from decaying leaves. The substrate is typically fine sand blanketed by leaf litter, and light penetration is minimal under the dense forest canopy.

decorPreferences: dark substrate (fine sand or dark gravel)
driftwood and dried leaf litter for tannins
dense planting around periphery (Amazon swords, Java fern, Vallisneria)

diet.notes: Accepts quality flake and micro-granule foods as a staple. Regular offerings of small live and frozen foods — baby brine shrimp, daphnia, micro-worms, and cyclops — are important for vibrant red coloration and long-term health. Color-enhancing foods containing krill or astaxanthin are beneficial. Small mouth size requires appropriately sized particles.

avoidWith: boisterous or fast-moving fish that outcompete at feeding
baby shrimp and fish fry (predation risk)

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Soulkeeper, 2034x1210. Tack-sharp lateral view; vivid firehead red extends from snout onto the operculum and hyaline anal fin is clear, with a crisp black-and-white checkerboard caudal fin — all key diagnostic features.
Photo by -serwacy01-, 5184x3456. Sharp group of three individuals against a dark background showing the schooling nature; red heads and checkerboard caudal fins clearly visible.
Author not stated on file, 1632x1224. Full shoal in a planted display tank, representing how the species is kept; red heads and striped caudals visible though fish are smaller in frame.

summary: The Firehead Tetra (Hemigrammus bleheri) is one of the most commonly traded members of the rummy-nose complex, distinguished by intense red coloration extending from the snout well onto the gill cover. It is a schooling blackwater species that needs soft, acidic water and groups of at least ten to display its characteristic tight-formation schooling.
careNotes:
The Firehead Tetra (Hemigrammus bleheri) is one of the species most commonly sold under the trade name 'rummy-nose tetra,' and it is generally considered the most brightly colored of the three closely related species that share that name. Buyers should be aware that three distinct species are sold interchangeably as rummy-nose tetras, and they differ in the extent of their red coloration. The Common Rummy-Nose (Hemigrammus rhodostomus) has red confined to the snout that does not extend past the eye. The Firehead Tetra has the most vivid red, which extends well onto the operculum (the bony gill cover) and into the humeral region behind it — the source of the 'firehead' name. The False Rummy-Nose (Petitella georgiae) shows the least red, and can be identified by a distinctive black stripe that extends forward from the base of the tail. The Firehead Tetra can also be distinguished from P. georgiae by its anal fin, which is hyaline (completely clear).

This species is widely regarded as a sensitive water-quality indicator. Aquarists commonly use the caudal fin pattern — a bold black-and-white checkerboard — and the red head coloration as a 'barometer' of tank conditions: fading of the red or pallor in the caudal bars tends to be a reliable early warning of elevated nitrates, ammonia, or temperature stress. Nitrates are generally advised to be kept below 20 ppm, supported by regular water changes of 25–30% weekly.

Keep in groups of 10 or more; smaller groups tend to show stress, reduced color, and corner-hugging behavior. The characteristic tight schooling formation only emerges in adequate numbers. A tank of at least 80 L with a 90 cm footprint is recommended, and longer tanks are preferable to allow schooling runs across open water. Soft, acidic, tannin-stained water (pH 5.5–7.0, gH 2–15) replicates the blackwater conditions of their native Rio Negro and Rio Meta biotopes. Peat filtration or commercially prepared blackwater extract — such as Indian almond leaves or alder cones — is strongly advised, as colors tend to fade noticeably in alkaline or hard water. Temperature should be kept stable at 23–28°C; sudden fluctuations have been observed to cause immediate color loss and immune suppression.

The species was described by Géry and Mahnert in 1986 and named in honor of Heiko Bleher, the German aquarist and explorer who first collected and shipped specimens for scientific identification. A 2020 molecular phylogenetic study reclassified it to the genus Petitella (as Petitella bleheri), placing it as a sister species to P. georgiae; many hobbyist and trade sources continue to use the original Hemigrammus bleheri name.
breedingNotes: The Firehead Tetra is a moderately challenging egg scatterer to breed in captivity. A dedicated breeding tank of approximately 40–60 L is recommended, set up with very soft water (gH 1–5), pH 5.5–6.5, and temperature raised slightly to 26–28°C. Fine-leaved plants such as Java moss or hornwort, or synthetic spawning mops, provide spawning sites, and a mesh grid over the tank bottom helps protect eggs from the egg-eating parents. Dim lighting is important throughout the process. Condition the breeding pair or small group with live and frozen foods for one to two weeks prior to spawning. Females scatter small, adhesive eggs among the plants; adults should be removed promptly after spawning. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–36 hours. Fry are sensitive to light initially and the tank should be kept dark during early development. Feed with infusoria or commercial fry foods for the first week, progressing to microworms and baby brine shrimp nauplii as they grow. Breeding in captivity is achievable, though it remains moderately challenging; much of the commercially available stock is sourced from farm operations in Southeast Asia or from wild collection in South America.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Hemigrammus bleheri
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided water parameters (23–26°C, pH 5.5–7.0, hardness 2–15°H), minimum tank length (90 cm), group size (10+), diet, breeding protocol, and key distinguishing features of H. bleheri vs rhodostomus and P. georgiae (red extending past gill covers, hyaline anal fin).

additional.name: FishBase — Petitella bleheri (syn. Hemigrammus bleheri)
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (3.6 cm SL), temperature (23–26°C), pH (5.0–6.0), hardness (5–12 dH), native range (Rio Negro and Rio Meta basins; Brazil, Colombia, Venezuela), trophic level 3.1 (omnivore), taxonomy (Characiformes). Notes 2020 reclassification to Petitella bleheri under Acestrorhamphidae; per task brief family listed as Characidae (traditional hobbyist convention).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Rummy-Nose Tetra Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade care guide. Confirmed temperature 23–29°C, adult size ~5 cm, minimum tank 20 gal (~75 L), schooling group 8–12, lifespan up to 5+ years, diet (baby brine shrimp, nano pellets, daphnia, color-enhancing foods), compatibility with peaceful community fish. Identified three-species complex (H. rhodostomus, H. bleheri, P. georgiae). Used 80 L / 90 cm minimum per SeriouslyFish as more species-specific guidance.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Hemigrammus bleheri
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (middle Rio Negro and upper/middle Rio Orinoco basins; Brazil, Colombia, Venezuela), etymology (named for Heiko Bleher), 2020 molecular reclassification to Petitella bleheri, distinguishing features (red to humeral region, hyaline anal fin, caudal bar does not extend far forward), and ornamental trade export figures (7.1 million specimens from Amazonas state 2006–2015).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Rummy-Nose Tetra (lifespan supplementary)
additional.notes: Aquarium Breeder detailed guide confirmed lifespan range 5–8 years in well-maintained aquaria; corroborated diet, schooling behavior, and blackwater biotope preferences.

fish.breedingNotes: An egg scatterer that requires a dedicated soft-water breeding tank (pH 5.5–6.5, gH 1–5, 26–28°C). Adults must be removed immediately after spawning to prevent egg predation. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–36 hours; fry begin on infusoria, then progress to microworms and baby brine shrimp nauplii. Captive breeding is achievable but moderately challenging.
